input_text:
Voyager 1 began its journey from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. This deployment occurred sixteen days after its companion, Voyager 2, a difference that might have surprised observers since NASA had planned unique trajectories for the two probes. The primary mission, which spanned five years, launched aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket. The goal of this venture was to execute close flybys of Jupiter and Saturn, taking advantage of a rare planetary alignment that repeats roughly every 176 years.

The probe arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During its visit, it successfully transmitted the first high-resolution visuals showing the planet’s faint ring structure and documented active volcanoes on its moon, Io, marking the initial observation of volcanism beyond Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 reached Saturn, sending back breathtaking images of its rings and close-up views of Titan. The trajectory toward Titan subsequently directed Voyager 1 past the plane of the solar system, concluding its planetary science phase while simultaneously setting a course for interstellar space.

Following a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 aimed its cameras back towards Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing an image of our planet when it was about 6 billion kilometers away. This singular image, which appeared as a single pixel against the Sun's weak radiation, achieved lasting renown as the Pale Blue Dot. This represented the final photograph taken by the spacecraft before the cameras were powered down to conserve its dwindling power supply.

On August 25, 2012, Voyager 1 entered the heliopause, thereby becoming the first human-made object to reach interstellar space. The mission carries the Golden Record—a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated by Sagan—which includes greetings in 55 languages, selected music, and visuals meant for any civilization it might encounter. Its operation is sustained by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators powered by plutonium-238, whose half-life of 87.7 years has gradually diminished the spacecraft's energy.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is approximately 25 billion kilometers from Earth, maintaining a velocity approaching 61,000 kilometers per hour. Additionally, projections indicate that by November 2026, it will become the first probe to traverse a distance equivalent to one light-day.
output_text:
Voyager 1 began its voyage from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. This deployment occurred sixteen days after its companion, Voyager 2, a difference that might have surprised observers since NASA had planned unique paths for the two probes. The primary mission, which spanned five years, launched aboard a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket. The venture's objective was to conduct close flybys of Jupiter and Saturn, capitalizing on a rare planetary alignment that recurs approximately every 176 years.

The probe encountered Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this visit, it successfully relayed the first high-resolution images revealing the planet’s faint ring structure and documented active volcanoes on its moon, Io, which marked the initial observation of volcanism outside of Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 reached Saturn, transmitting magnificent photographs of its rings and detailed views of Titan. The subsequent trajectory toward Titan guided Voyager 1 beyond the plane of the solar system, concluding its phase of planetary science while simultaneously setting a course toward interstellar space.

Following a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 directed its cameras back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, capturing an image of our planet when it was situated about 6 billion kilometers away. This solitary picture, which appeared as a single pixel against the Sun's diminished radiation, gained enduring fame as the Pale Blue Dot. This photograph represented the final capture by the spacecraft before the cameras were shut down to conserve its waning power supply.

On August 25, 2012, Voyager 1 entered the heliopause, thus becoming the first human-made object to enter interstellar space. The mission includes the Golden Record—a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc assembled by Sagan—which contains greetings in 55 languages, chosen music, and visuals intended for any civilization it might encounter. Its ongoing operation is sustained by three radioisotope thermoelectric generators powered by plutonium-238, whose half-life of 87.7 years has gradually reduced the spacecraft's energy.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is approximately 25 billion kilometers distant from Earth, maintaining a velocity nearing 61,000 kilometers per hour. Furthermore, projections suggest that by November 2026, it will become the first probe to cover a distance equivalent to one light-day.
